Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/cc-switch-cli.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ugv5biit...

Analyzing cc-switch-cli AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ugv5biit) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines two sources based on the architecture, both pointing to GitHub release binaries for the project. These URLs are static and point directly to versioned release assets on the official upstream repository (github.com/SaladDay/cc-switch-cli), which is consistent with standard and safe sourcing practices. The `sha256sums` are explicitly provided and match the respective binaries, preventing tampering during download.

No `pkgver()` function is defined, so no dynamic version calculation occurs. The top-level scope contains no command substitutions, immediate execution statements, or network operations beyond what's declared in `source`. The `package()` function is irrelevant here, as it is not executed under `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only source the PKGBUILD and fetch the listed sources, which are safe operations in this context.

All operations are limited to downloading verifiable, static release binaries from the official upstream. There is no evidence of dynamic code evaluation, data exfiltration, or remote payload execution during sourcing. The file adheres to standard AUR packaging practices and poses no risk during the described narrow safety gate.
</details>
<summary>
Safe sourcing and static, verifiable sources
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe sourcing and static, verifiable sources[/]

makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for a CLI tool that supports multiple AI models and fetches pre-built binaries from the project's official GitHub releases page. The sources are architecture-specific tarballs hosted at predictable URLs under the upstream repository (SaladDay/cc-switch-cli), which is consistent with standard packaging practices for binary distributions. The sha256 checksums are provided for both x86_64 and aarch64 variants, ensuring integrity verification of the downloaded binaries.

The package function installs the binary `cc-switch` with appropriate permissions into `/usr/bin/cc-switch`, which is standard behavior. There are no network requests, dynamic downloads, or execution of fetched code during installation. The package does not use `eval`, `curl|bash`, `git pull`, or other risky patterns. All operations are transparent and confined to the expected scope of installing a single binary.

While the tool itself may communicate with external AI services at runtime (as implied by its purpose), that is upstream application functionality, not a supply-chain compromise. There is no evidence of code injection, obfuscation, or unauthorized system modifications in the PKGBUILD.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with verified GitHub sources
</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only variable assignments in its global scope (no code execution during sourcing) and no `pkgver()` function. The `source` arrays point to pinned GitHub release tarballs with explicit SHA256 checksums. No dangerous commands (curl, wget, eval, base64, etc.) appear at any scope. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only download the specified tarballs (if not cached) and parse the variable definitions—there is no mechanism for malicious execution.
</details>
<summary>PKGBUILD is safe for source fetch and parsing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The `package()` function simply installs the binary to `/usr/bin/` with proper executable permissions. There are no obfuscated commands, unexpected network requests, or any operations that deviate from normal packaging. No evidence of supply-chain attack or malicious behavior.
</details>

Final Status: SAFE


No issues found.

data.reasoning: This is a prebuilt binary package that fetches architecture-specific release tarballs from the official upstream GitHub repository (SaladDay/cc-switch-cli) with pinned SHA256 checksums. The package() function only installs the binary to /usr/bin with standard permissions. There is no pkgver() function, no .install hooks, no prepare/build/check functions, and no dynamic code execution, network calls, or obfuscation. Both models independently reached the same verdict with consistent and accurate reasoning. The only attack surface is the binary itself, which is opaque and cannot be statically reviewed in an AUR audit, but it is integrity-pinned to an official source.
coverage_issues: Both models reviewed only the PKGBUILD (1 file). This is appropriate for a binary package since there are no scripts or install hooks in the repo, but neither explicitly confirmed the absence of a .install file or other auxiliary files in the AUR repo tree.
false_negatives: Neither model addressed that the actual binary contents are unauditable — the package installs a prebuilt executable from GitHub releases that could itself contain malicious code. This is an inherent and accepted limitation for binary AUR packages, but it should be acknowledged rather than implied to be fully safe.
learnings: For binary packages (prebuilt -bin style), a single-file PKGBUILD review is sufficient and the 'shallow' trigger is a false alarm — the tool could detect that no other reviewable files exist in the repo and suppress the shallow warning.
Models should explicitly enumerate the AUR repo tree (e.g. confirm no .install hook, no patch files) to distinguish 'reviewed all available files' from 'reviewed only one of several files'.

Models should note the inherent limitation that prebuilt binaries cannot be statically audited, so the 'safe' verdict reflects supply-chain integrity (pinned checksums + official source) rather than binary content safety.
Both models gave well-reasoned, accurate assessments correctly distinguishing runtime AI-service communication (upstream app behavior) from supply-chain risk — this is a good pattern to reinforce.

data.reasoning: Both models correctly identified the PKGBUILD as safe due to pinned GitHub sources, verified checksums, and standard binary installation without dangerous commands or dynamic execution risks.
coverage_issues: Both models failed to review potential .install hooks or other package scripts. The tool should prioritize reviewing all executable scripts referenced in PKGBUILD.
false_negatives: Potential runtime risks (network access by the binary) were noted but correctly deemed out-of-scope for AUR audits.
learnings: File selection must prioritize all executable package scripts (e.g., .install hooks) regardless of model discretion
Add explicit checks for hook scripts during initial file selection
Improve model guidance to distinguish between build-time risks (in-scope) and runtime behavior (out-of-scope)
Implement mandatory review of any file referenced in PKGBUILD functions (package(), prepare(), etc.)
